disclaimer: Published research cited below. This is not medical advice. Correlation in studies does not prove any single bike causes injury.

stats.headline: Pediatric e-bike trauma linked to speed factors
stats.detail: Rady Children's-led study (Feb 2026) examines how speed relates to e-bike injury severity in children and teens, as ED visits continue to rise.
stats.does_not_mean: Does not mean every e-bike is dangerous — it highlights speed as a risk factor worth weighing for a 12-year-old.

stats.headline: CPSC tracks e-bike deaths and injuries nationally
stats.detail: CPSC micromobility report (2017–2024) documents deaths, injuries, and hazard patterns across e-bikes and related products.
stats.does_not_mean: Does not mean your neighborhood has the same rate — national data sets context, not personal odds.

stats.headline: Children's hospitals report rising pediatric e-bike injuries
stats.detail: Multiple pediatric trauma centers report sharp increases in e-bike-related injuries among kids and teens, with many crashes involving vehicles.
stats.does_not_mean: Does not mean walking or a pedal bike is risk-free — it means e-bike speed and traffic exposure need planning.
stats.source: Children's Hospital Colorado / national pediatric reports

glossary.Class 1: Pedal-assist only, stops assisting at 20 mph
glossary.Class 2: Throttle allowed, motor stops at 20 mph
glossary.Class 3: Pedal-assist, stops at 28 mph — illegal to operate under age 16 in California
glossary.ED visit: Emergency department visit — serious but not the same as a fatality
